region_of_origin: Global (widely cultivated; commonly consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry covers raw snow pea; boiled preparation is not separately coded. Closest parent match used; boiling method documented in classification.
ontology.foodex2_description: Snow peas (Pisum sativum var. saccharatum) — pod vegetable, boiled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 base term for snow pea used; boiled preparation facet applied by convention. Code A00LC corresponds to snow peas in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used as 'boiled' is a preparation method, not a distinct food item in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for boiled snow pea as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of boiled snow peas is approximately 85 g (about 3/4 cup), as commonly plated in Singapore hawker and home-cooked meals.

nutrient_source.title: Snow Peas, Boiled — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09020012 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for boiled snow pea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 22 is estimated from the closest analogous leguminous pod vegetable (green peas, boiled, GI ≈ 22–48 range; low end used for snow pea given lower starch density and higher fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (22) × carbohydrate_g per serving (5.0 g × 0.85 serving = 4.25 g) / 100 = 0.94, rounded to 0.9. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with the food's macronutrient profile.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (406 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled snow peas are an excellent choice for hypertension management due to their near-zero sodium content and high potassium (406 mg/100 g), which supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary guidelines; avoid adding soy sauce or salty condiments after cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.0 g/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~22)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled snow peas are suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management; their low carbohydrate content, moderate fibre, and estimated low glycemic index (~22) result in a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving, supporting stable postprandial blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.2 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled snow peas are cardioprotective: negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol minimise atherogenic risk, while high potassium (406 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g) support healthy blood pressure and lipid profiles, making them a recommended vegetable for cardiovascular disease prevention diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (~60 mg/100 g) with antioxidant properties, but limited omega-3 fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled snow peas provide meaningful vitamin C (~60 mg/100 g), which may reduce oxidative stress relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, but they lack omega-3 fatty acids and other primary anti-inflammatory nutrients; they are a supportive but not sufficient standalone food for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled snow peas (Pisum sativum var. saccharatum) are the edible flat pods of the snow pea plant, cooked by brief immersion in boiling water until tender-crisp. The result is a bright green, lightly sweet, and mildly grassy-flavoured vegetable with a satisfying snap. Snow peas are widely consumed across Singapore, East Asia, and Southeast Asia as a side dish, stir-fry component, or salad ingredient. Nutritionally, boiled snow peas are low in calories (40 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.2 g), and provide a moderate amount of plant protein (3.2 g/100 g) alongside 5.0 g of carbohydrate and 2.8 g of dietary fibre per 100 g. Their sodium content is negligible at 1 mg/100 g, while potassium is notably high at 406 mg/100 g, making them an excellent DASH-diet food for blood pressure management. The estimated glycemic index is low (~22), and glycemic load per typical serving is under 1, making them suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. Vitamin C content is approximately 60 mg/100 g, contributing antioxidant benefit. Boiled snow peas are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, with no known common allergens. They are commonly served as a side vegetable in Singaporean home cooking and hawker-style meals.
common_user_queries: Are snow peas good for diabetics?
How many calories in boiled snow peas?
Can I eat snow peas if I have high blood pressure?
What is the potassium content of snow peas?
Snow pea vs sugar snap pea nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09020012, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source (University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care 2008), which reduces GI confidence to medium; overall record confidence is set to high given the exact nutrient source match and comprehensive enrichment.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for boiled snow pea is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F09020012. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry for F09020012; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11300, snow peas raw) was consulted as a secondary reference but values were not substituted to preserve source integrity. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0: confirmed zero for a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. 5. saturated_fat_g (0.03 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11301 (snow peas, boiled, drained) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is an adapted value and is documented accordingly. 6. vitamin_c_mg (60.0 mg) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11301 (snow peas, boiled) as the HPB source does not report vitamin C; this value is consistent with published literature for boiled snow peas. 7. GI of 22 is an analogous estimate based on green peas (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care 2008 international GI tables; no direct GI entry for snow pea exists in the approved GI database. 8. food_group corrected from input value 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Vegetables' as snow peas are a pod vegetable; the input classification appears to be a data entry error. 9. keto dietary tag from input removed: snow peas at 5.0 g carbohydrate/100 g are borderline for strict ketogenic diets and the tag is not clinically defensible without portion qualification.
